title: Structure Editor Functions that Change the Active Path
breadcrumbs: Structure Editor Functions that Change the Active Path
text: Structure Editor Functions that Change the Active Path Structure editor functions that change the field that has focus can change the active path, as can ^STORE and ^ACCEPT. ^FIRST, ^LAST, ^NEXT, or ^PREV used in combination with ^FIELD. For each pair, this includes both the function pair (for example, ^NEXT^FIELD) and combined function (for example, ^NEXT_FIELD). These functions act on the field node (that is, the field that is being left). ^FIRST, ^LAST, ^NEXT, or ^PREV used in combination with ^OCCURRENCE or ^OCC_WINDOW. For each pair, this includes both the function pair (for example, ^NEXT^OCCURRENCE) and combined function (for example, ^NEXT_OCC). These functions act on the last field node (that is, the field that is being left) and the last entity node (that is, the occurrence that is being left). ^CURSOR_DOWN, ^CURSOR_UP, ^CURSOR_FAST_UP, and ^CURSOR_FAST_DOWN, in character mode. These functions act on the last field node (that is, the field that is being left) and the last entity node (that is, the occurrence that is being left), if appropriate. ^STORE and ^ACCEPT. These functions act on all the nodes on the active path (that is, the last field and all entity occurrences). Related Links Active Path for Runtime Processing The active path determines where processing is applied in a runtime component, and the order in which it occurs. It leads from the current occurrence of the outermost entity down to the current occurrence of the innermost entity. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
